summary: The main claim is: The dependence of values on concepts means that concept alignment is a prerequisite for value alignment -- agents need to align their representation of a situation with that of humans in order to successfully align their values.
Value alignment is essential for building AI systems that can safely and reliably interact with people.
The main claim is: Therefore, inspired by satisficing, we introduce a novel concept: ℵ -aspiring agent .
In the MAB environment, the problem was that the algorithm was too pessimistic about what is feasible because of too low Q values.

The main claim is: The prospect of sentient AI is particularly daunting as 20% of people think that some AIs are already sentient; 10% think ChatGPT is sentient; and 69% support a ban on the development of sentient AIs.
A screenshot of a graph Support and opposition to policies related to sentient AI from the 2023 AIMS main and 2023 AIMS supplemental survey.
The main claim is: Public discussions about catastrophic risks from general AI systems are often derailed by using the word “intelligence”.
I would advocate for using the term “capabilities” or “competence” instead of “intelligence” when discussing catastrophic risks from AI, because this is what the concerns are really about.
The main claim is: Perhaps throwing a large corpus of formal maths proofs in there.
It should be optimized purely for ZFC formal theorem proving.

top_comment.author: Razied
top_comment.text:
I think the general idea of "make a math specialized LLM to help alignment" is promising. We'd like to train AIs that gain in capability unevenly, staying dumb at manipulating humans while being superhuman at math proofs (like the stereotypically autistic math researcher). While this can't work for arbitrarily high levels of capability, I agree that this is where the hope lies. I think this math-specialized LLM together with strong boxing techniques, interpretability techniques, indepedent human verification of results, and a few more hacks[1] of this sort have a shot at enabling us to wield a weakly superhuman AI to solve alignment. But for this to work one of the AI safety labs have to have enough of a capabilities advantage to pay the alignment tax.

 1. ^
    
    Like training an ensemble of these models at the same time, then seeing if the models disagree on their outputs. This comes from the intuition that the exact same mesaoptimizer will not develop inside multiple different models, and so they won't be able to hide the exact same subtle errors inside their proofs. 
    
    Or for every next-token prediction we randomly select one of the models from the ensemble, so all models would need to cooperate in order to hide bad outcomes in the output, making it harder for them.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: I think the general idea of "make a math specialized LLM to help alignment" is promising.

summary: The main claim is: I think the terms "AI Alignment" and "AI existential safety" are often used interchangeably, leading the ideas to be conflated.

The problem with (1) is that it is too broad, and invites the response: "Isn't that what most/all AI research is about?" The problem with (3) is that it suggests that (Intent) Alignment is the one-and-only way to increase AI existential sa…
The main claim is: Let's say this agent found a powerful action that the metric didn't recognize as powerful.
The RL agent can maximize its reward by being deceptive and hiding that the action is powerful.
The main claim is: bbp paper1: 27 seconds of compute time for 0.1 seconds of biological time for 1?
The new brain, or neocortex, is a single large organ.
The main claim is: Rebuttal: During training, run the human net for pnsteps before calling the reporter.
+ Since the encoder is bad, the reporter is bad; since the reporter is bad, the encoder stays bad.
The main claim is: FAI could destroy all weapons of mass destruction even if the vast majority of humans disagree.
All mentally healthy humans don't want to die.
The main claim is: High standards of truthfulness could be less costly for AI systems than for humans for several reasons.
New standards of truthfulness would only apply to AI systems and would not restrict human speech.

The main claim is: But I think the Adversarial Offer is one of the more convincing "counterexamples" to CDT.
Most people on this forum are probably already convinced that (orthodox, two-boxing) CDT should be rejected.
The main claim is: At the time of the conversation, Daniel was one of these researchers, who have what we’ll call “security mindset”.
